claim: Superconducting: ~70% chance of reaching fault-tolerant, useful-scale QC (~10-15 yr). Most mature, most capital, fastest gates, error correction already improving with scale; multiple serious players de-risk it. Loose forecast, independent per-path odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if modular interconnect and logical scaling keep working; down if surface-code overhead proves intractable

claim: Trapped ion: ~60%. Highest demonstrated quality (accuracy, coherence, ~2:1 physical-to-logical ratio); open question is scaling qubit count, not quality. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if a chip-based ion system scales past hundreds of qubits with fidelity intact

claim: Neutral atom: ~58%. Fastest recent progress, leads in error-corrected qubit count, cheap identical atoms, software-reconfigurable connectivity; held back mainly by relative youth. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if sustained large logical-qubit operation at higher fidelity is demonstrated

claim: Silicon spin: ~45%. Best manufacturing/scaling story (rides the semiconductor industry), just hit top-tier quality on an industrial line; but fewest qubits so far and no logical-qubit demo yet. High variance. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if a first credible logical qubit is built on silicon spin

claim: Photonic: ~35%. Room-temperature operation and native networking are real advantages, but gated almost entirely by unsolved optical loss; many gates probabilistic, needing heavy redundancy. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if optical loss drops below the fault-tolerance threshold with a logical qubit demonstrated

claim: Topological: ~15%. A bet on an unproven physics claim; the existence of the qubit itself is disputed by much of the community; low expected value precisely because the existence question is open. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: independent reproduction of an accepted topological qubit; could collapse toward 0 or jump materially

claim: Annealing/D-Wave gate-model pivot: ~30% (aside, not ranked head-to-head). Present-day annealing optimization usefulness is a separate, already-banked win answering a different question. Loose forecast, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if the gate-model line delivers logical qubits on its roadmap

claim: Author thesis (tracked forecast): the long-term endpoint of quantum computing is optical/photonic rather than electronic. The author's conviction after years of research, which currently runs ahead of the demonstrated evidence (which ranks photonic lower today, ~35%); conviction and demonstration are different columns of the ledger.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: photonic optical loss below the fault-tolerance threshold with a working logical qubit; if it hits, the photonic probability (QCF5) revises up materially

claim: NV centers (diamond): ~12%. Physics is strong but the case for scalable general-purpose computing is weak — placing and wiring many NV qubits accurately in diamond is the unsolved problem, and the field has largely converged on NV as excellent for sensing, networking, and communication rather than a path to a general fault-tolerant computer. The ~12% measures the fault-tolerant, useful-scale COMPUTING question specifically; NV may succeed strongly in a different lane (sensing/networking) that this number does not measure. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if a credible multi-qubit error-corrected NV processor architecture with a real scaling path appears (not another sensing/networking milestone)

claim: III-V quantum dots (optically-active, photon sources): ~10-12%. Physics is strong but as a route to a general fault-tolerant computer it is weak — dot-to-dot variability and the fact they function as a COMPONENT feeding photonic/network architectures rather than a self-contained processor. The ~10-12% measures the fault-tolerant, useful-scale COMPUTING question specifically; III-V dots may succeed strongly in a different lane (best-in-class on-demand single-photon sources; spin-photon interfaces for networking) that this number does not measure. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if a credible scalable processor architecture built on III-V quantum dots appears (not merely an improved photon-source benchmark)

claim: Hefei anchors China's effort: the National Laboratory for Quantum Information Sciences (set in motion after Xi's 2016 USTC visit) and the 'Quantum Avenue' cluster on Yunfei Road (30+ companies). Verified construction figure ~CNY 7B (~$1B) Phase 1 per MERICS; 'world's largest facility' is a reported characterization.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (MERICS/SCMP/ITIF/china.org.cn)

claim: Pan Jianwei / USTC milestones: Micius satellite first space-to-ground QKD + ~2,032 km Beijing-Shanghai QKD backbone; Jiuzhang photonic boson-sampling advantage (3.0 ~255 photons, 2023); Zuchongzhi superconducting processors (62 to ~176 qubits). Communication and sampling-advantage milestones, not fault-tolerant computing.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (CAS/USCC/USTC)

claim: Private pullback, state effort still rising: Alibaba (Nov 2023) and Baidu (Jan 2024) closed their quantum labs and pivoted to AI, but the overall Chinese effort grew — state-aligned specialists (Origin Quantum, QuantumCTek) took over commercially, the 2026 15th Five-Year Plan names quantum an industrial priority, a national guidance fund earmarked ~CNY 121.8B, and Q1-2026 financing (~CNY 2.2B) nearly matched all of 2025.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (DCD/IEEE Spectrum/HPCwire/quantumzeitgeist/The Quantum Insider)

claim: Circulating dollar figures for China's quantum spend are unreliable and unverifiable (the most-quoted ~$15.3B traces to one caveated 2022 McKinsey estimate; same-lineage figures range to $150B; an insider suggests perhaps a third). This dossier does NOT rest on any headline spend number and argues instead from tempo, structure and purchasing-power (see CN9).
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (McKinsey via postquantum analysis); not used as a load-bearing claim

claim: Documented Chinese elite-university drive (rival framing only): the Ministry of Education named 12 universities to 'Schools of Future Technology' tasked explicitly to RIVAL MIT and Stanford in science and technology; the Double First-Class initiative (双一流, 2015; 2017 lists) named 42 'world-class' universities and 465 first-class disciplines across 140 institutions, aiming to be a higher-education powerhouse by 2050.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (SCMP/Wikipedia/THE/University World News)

claim: Author argument (the author's view): the question is not whether the West has already lost, but whether US/allied quantum investment, institutions and talent pipelines are large enough and EARLY enough to keep pace with a still-accelerating two-decade Chinese program — judged on tempo and structure, not on unreliable dollar totals. A wake-up call, not an obituary; counter-evidence held in the same breath — dollar figures are unreliable in either direction, much of China's lead is in communication, and most assessments still put the US ahead in quantum computing.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: eased if the US/allies sustain a reauthorized fully-funded national quantum program and are first to fault-tolerant useful-scale QC; sharpened if China is first to fault-tolerant useful-scale QC or fields an operational unbreakable continental quantum network while Western funding stays stop-start

claim: China runs large international STEM talent-recruitment programs (the Thousand Talents Plan and rebranded successors), courting researchers abroad with multi-year packages; e.g. a 2024 program reportedly approached 149 professors (~a fifth of faculty) at South Korea's KAIST. The people side of the decades-scale STEM bet.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (Korea Herald/Stanford FSI/Wikipedia)

claim: US response lag: the US National Quantum Initiative (2018) saw its core R&D authorization lapse on 30 Sep 2023, with reauthorization stalled in committee through 2024 and only re-advancing in 2026; a federal panel urged a 'Quantum First by 2030' goal (Nov 2025) and industry (Microsoft) warned the US risks falling behind and urged a larger STEM-talent pipeline.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (FedScoop/The Quantum Insider/Congress.gov/CNBC)

claim: Why dollar comparisons mislead: lower Chinese labour/materials/fabrication/construction costs mean each R&D dollar buys more — ITIF estimates ~2.3 researchers per $100k R&D in China (>2x the US), and on a PPP basis China has drawn level with or passed US total R&D (OECD: 2024 ~$1.03T vs $1.01T). USD-to-USD understates effective Chinese output, so the dossier argues from tempo/structure/PPP rather than headline dollars.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (ITIF/OECD via Science)

claim: Macro base rate (EV/battery S-curve): from a near-zero base 15-20 years ago, Chinese firms now install ~70-75% of the world's EV batteries (CATL ~38% global, BYD ~17%); LFP >80% of domestic installs; sodium-ion commercializing while solid-state targets ~2027. A manufacturing/scale-up trajectory, cited as the 'don't bet against the Chinese S-curve' base rate.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (IEA/CnEVPost/Carbon Credits)

claim: China scientific-publication record: #1 in total publications (2024, first since US passed UK 1948); #1 in top-1% most-cited papers since ~2019 (27.2% vs US 24.9%, 2018-20); #1 in Nature Index natural sciences (2023) with physics/chemistry strength and CAS/USTC/Tsinghua among top institutions. Caveats: high self-citation, paper-mill/fraud concerns, quantity-vs-quality debate; some quality-weighted measures still favor the US.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (Science/AAAS/Nature/NISTEP/WIPO GII/CEPR)

claim: AI JUDGMENT (Opus 4.8, independent experiment; analysis/opinion, NOT a verified claim and NOT an authority): §04's thesis is fairly stated to modestly understated — correctly bounded as a question not a verdict and conceding the current US computing lead, but under-deploying its strongest evidence (China's leading high-impact physics output + the EV S-curve base rate); a clean 'China wins fault-tolerant QC' prediction is NOT supported given Western near-term fault-tolerance leads and remaining basic-science gates. Offered as one data point to weigh.
verifier: AI analysis/opinion — not mechanically verifiable; would shift toward understated if remaining QC gates prove mostly engineering, or toward overstated if the West sustains a funded fault-tolerance lead

claim: CHIPS & Science Act science-side underfunding: NSF received $9.06B in FY24 vs $15.64B authorized (~42% short); combined NSF/NIST/DOE-Science gap >$8B (FY24-25); the NSF technology directorate (TIP) got ~$410M of ~$20B authorized. The US legislated a science surge then did not appropriate it.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (FAS/CSIS/AEI/ITIF)

claim: US semiconductor reshoring — mixed record: >$630B committed across ~140 projects, ~500k jobs since 2020 (SIA); but US fab costs 30-50% above Asia, high-skilled labour short, TSMC Arizona slipped 2026 to 2028; friend-shoring (US-India fabs) adds allied capacity. Reshoring works partially and at a premium.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (SIA/Manufacturing Dive/Stimson/SAIS/CFR)

claim: US high-skilled STEM immigration: estimated ~1.4M shortage of technicians/scientists/engineers by 2030 while China roughly doubles STEM PhDs; reform proposals (Schedule A for AI, advanced-STEM green-card and stay-rate pathways) vs counter-currents (e.g. 2025 $100k H-1B fee argued to be self-defeating).
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (CNAS/Brookings/IFP/Fwd.us/Lawfare)

claim: RECOMMENDATION (policy argument, not verified): Pillar 1 — regain scientific-research leadership by fully and STABLY (multi-year) funding the basic-science surge already authorized under CHIPS, reauthorizing and funding the NQI, and protecting the NSF basic-science core. Trade-offs: budget cost, appropriations politics, risk of securitizing/distorting basic science.
verifier: policy opinion — open argument; signpost: progress if CHIPS science appropriations close toward authorization and NQI is sustained multi-year

claim: RECOMMENDATION (policy argument): Pillar 2 — build a competitive industrialization S-curve by pairing supply-side investment with durable demand-side pull (procurement/deployment), prioritizing scale-compounding sectors (batteries, advanced packaging, quantum-enabling fab), and treating workforce as the binding constraint — not brute-force reshoring alone, given the 30-50% US cost premium. Trade-offs: cost, time, risk of subsidizing uncompetitive industries.
verifier: policy opinion — open argument; signpost: a domestic industry sustainably riding its own learning/cost-down curve

claim: RECOMMENDATION (policy argument): on China's industrial capacity, run a managed PORTFOLIO matched to sector security-criticality — friend-shoring/allied supply chains, buy-and-improve/leveraging interdependence, reshoring incentives, tariffs/industrial policy, talent & immigration, public-private fabs, standards/IP leadership, narrow export controls/selective decoupling — each an option with explicit costs; cheapest highest-leverage moves (talent, stable science funding, allies) first; coercive tools narrow and costly. Options analysis, NOT operational economic-warfare tactics.
verifier: policy opinion — open argument; signpost: a coherent portfolio rather than a single ideological lever

claim: RECOMMENDATION (policy argument): Pillar 3 — QC leadership is necessary but not sufficient and may be the wrong standalone target; convert the current US near-term fault-tolerance lead (per the avenues section) into durable advantage by strengthening the macro science+industry base beneath it, rather than treating QC as an isolated moonshot or over-indexing on one modality.
verifier: policy opinion — open argument; signpost: US QC lead sustained AND macro science/industry base strengthening together

claim: COUNTER-VIEW (labeled counter-argument): dominance may be the wrong goal — much frontier science is inherently international, a dominance frame that securitizes science can deter the collaboration and talent inflows that produce breakthroughs, and resilience + frontier presence + openness/standards leadership may be wiser and cheaper than striving to be #1 everywhere. Presented to keep the section strategy, not manifesto.
verifier: policy opinion / counter-argument — open; signpost: weigh resilience-and-frontier-presence outcomes against dominance-pursuit costs
